topic.what: Characterisation of a cancer-associated dysbiotic gastric microbiota featuring decreased microbial diversity and enrichment of oral-origin bacteria in gastric carcinoma compared to chronic gastritis
topic.how: Cohort study comparing gastric microbiome profiles via 16S rRNA gene sequencing between gastric cancer patients and chronic gastritis controls, with taxonomic and functional pathway analysis
topic.why_matters: Identifies a reproducible dysbiotic signature in gastric cancer characterised by reduced diversity and oral bacterial colonisation, supporting microbiome profiling as a potential avenue for cancer risk stratification

characteristics.dimension: microbial diversity in cancer vs gastritis
characteristics.what_it_measures: Alpha diversity difference between gastric cancer and chronic gastritis microbiomes
characteristics.value: significantly decreased diversity in gastric cancer compared to chronic gastritis
characteristics.vs_baseline: chronic gastritis retains higher microbial diversity; cancer microbiome is dominated by fewer taxa
characteristics.evidence: 16S rRNA sequencing with alpha diversity metrics
characteristics.context: Cross-sectional comparison; confounders include H. pylori status, diet, and medication

good_for: understanding microbial ecology in gastric cancer
not_for: causal inference about microbiome role in carcinogenesis

novelty: Demonstrated reproducible reduction in gastric microbial diversity as a feature of cancer-associated dysbiosis
Confirmed enrichment of oral-origin bacteria (Fusobacterium, Peptostreptococcus, Gemella) in gastric carcinoma microbiome
open_problems: Whether dysbiotic microbiota drives carcinogenesis or results from tumour microenvironment changes
Prospective validation of microbiome-based risk stratification for gastric cancer
Functional metabolomic impact of dysbiotic community on host tissue remains uncharacterised
Potential for microbiome-targeted interventions to reduce cancer risk is speculative
